briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 12, 2026. Based on the key climate-related news collected throughout the day, we will examine the reality of climate change and the efforts to address it from various perspectives.

sections.title: 🔴 Direct Impacts of Climate Change and Ecosystem Changes
sections.content: Today's briefing begins with the direct impacts of climate change on our ecosystems and the warning signs they present. Domestically, clear signals of ecosystem changes due to climate change are being observed, such as the discovery of a semi-mangrove species, previously unrecorded on the Korean Peninsula, along the Jeju coast, and the first-ever confirmation of subtropical and tropical plants in Korea [1, 8, 11]. Internationally, news has emerged of a surge in Sargassum in Miami and the devastation of coral reefs due to climate change [111, 113]. In the Amazon region, traces of climate change are also being revealed through tree rings [119]. With abnormal high temperatures in Phoenix in May [121] and the appearance of mosquitoes in Iceland, which was once insect-free [132], the accelerating impacts of climate change on ecosystems and local environments are severely manifesting worldwide [133]. These changes once again remind us of the reality of the climate crisis we face.

sections.title: ⚠️ Deepening Climate Crisis: Domestic and International Warnings and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Domestic and international warnings about extreme weather events due to climate change are growing louder, and adaptation efforts are urgently needed. The Korea Meteorological Administration has completely reorganized its heatwave advisories starting this summer, introducing 'Severe Heatwave Warnings' and 'Tropical Night Advisories,' and announced additional disaster text messages during heavy rainfall [15, 107, 108]. The Ministry of Climate is preparing AI and DT-based flood countermeasures, and the government is focusing on strengthening disaster response capabilities, including securing 1.04 billion tons of 'hidden water reservoirs' to prevent flood damage [3, 106]. Internationally, concerns about flooding among Essex residents [124] and abnormal high temperatures in Phoenix [121] have been reported. Warnings are also emerging that climate change could lead to health threats such as an increase in hantavirus hosts [115] or an increased risk of leptospirosis in Europe [117] [135, 137]. This situation demonstrates that the climate crisis is a complex issue that transcends simple environmental concerns, threatening the overall stability and safety of society.

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Efforts Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Various efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society are accelerating both domestically and internationally. In Jeju Island, a Climate Citizens' Assembly has been launched to achieve the '2035 Carbon Neutrality' goal, incorporating citizens' voices [18, 32, 55, 62]. Chungcheongnam-do is expanding its 'Carbon Up-Shoe' project, encouraging participation by offering benefits of up to 100,000 KRW for practicing carbon neutrality in daily life [19, 26, 27, 31, 34, 38, 40, 42, 43, 46, 47, 49, 51, 53, 54, 56, 57, 60, 61, 63]. Public institutions and corporations are also continuing their efforts, such as the National Health Insurance Service's electronic notification of health insurance premiums [20] and LS Cable & System's operation of an eco-friendly resource circulation supply chain [22, 109]. In the education sector, the Gyeongbuk Provincial Office of Education is piloting 'Sunlight Connection Schools' [24, 37], and local governments like Geoje City and Yeongju City are contributing to the spread of a carbon-neutral culture through outreach environmental education [23, 25, 29, 39, 45]. In Wando, an international forum on blue carbon utilizing seaweed was held to discuss carbon-neutral solutions [33, 59], strengthening the societal shift towards carbon neutrality.

sections.title: ⚡️ Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is a key driver for achieving carbon neutrality goals, and investment in it is continuously expanding. Wonkwang Electric Power [64, 101] and Kolon Benit [69] have completed their renewable energy business registrations and are actively pursuing direct Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs). KT has also signed a contract to directly supply renewable energy to contribute to the semiconductor ecosystem [66, 73]. Gyeonggi Province is supporting solar power installations through its 'Industrial Complex RE100 Transition Pilot Project' [92], and local governments such as Geumsan County [48, 77, 83, 84, 93, 98] and Jeollanam-do [75, 82, 88, 90, 96] are actively pursuing projects to expand renewable energy supply or requesting national funding. However, Europe's ENTSO-E has warned that expanding renewable energy is impossible without data center flexibility [70, 139]. Domestically, policy and technical challenges also abound, such as the abolition of REC (Renewable Energy Certificate) impacting the profitability of renewable energy pensions [100]. Amidst a complex situation where a power crisis for AI data centers is becoming a reality, and nuclear power and CCS (Carbon Capture and Storage) are emerging as alternatives [102], in-depth discussions are needed to build a stable renewable energy system [65, 91].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Societal Adaptation and International Cooperation in Response to Climate Change
sections.content: Societal adaptation efforts and international cooperation discussions regarding climate change are also actively underway. Domestically, the retail industry is adopting climate change as a new strategy, altering its product offerings. For instance, GS25 planned and exclusively launched umbrellas that can be used for both rain and sun, seeing a 79.5% increase in sales, demonstrating how convenience store hit products are changing in response to climate change [2, 6, 7, 14]. Meanwhile, the Climate Change Ambassador continued international cooperation by attending the 17th Petersburg Climate Dialogue [5]. At the WIPO Development Committee, international challenges are also becoming clearer, such as the reconfirmation of the gap between developed and developing countries during integrated discussions on intellectual property (IP) and climate change [10]. Policy responses from various countries are also noteworthy, such as New Zealand's push for climate law amendments to block private lawsuits holding companies accountable for greenhouse gas emissions [9, 136].

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, the impacts of climate change are becoming increasingly evident, and comprehensive societal efforts are underway both domestically and internationally to address them. Sustained attention and action are crucial to overcome the climate crisis. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Following last year's flood damage, including the swollen Dorimcheon stream in Seoul on July 17, the government is preparing for future floods by securing 1.04 billion tons of 'hidden water storage capacity' from agricultural reservoirs, dams, and estuary weirs. Additionally, 'urban flood forecasts' will be introduced for six districts, including the Gangnam Station area in Seoul, to inform residents of disaster risks.

Furthermore, 'urban flood forecasts' will be newly established for six districts, including the Gangnam Station area in Seoul.

articles.description: As heatwaves persist, with citizens hurrying to avoid the heat at Gwanghwamun Intersection in Seoul on June 27, 2024, the Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) is strengthening its weather warning system. This includes establishing new 'Severe Heatwave Warnings' and 'Tropical Night Advisories' in preparation for climate disasters this summer, marking the first revision of heatwave warnings in 18 years. The KMA announced these measures on the 12th, noting an an increase in average heatwave and tropical night days nationwide over the past five years.

summary.outlook: In preparation for climate disasters this summer, the Korea Meteorological Administration is reorganizing its heatwave warning system for the first time in 18 years, establishing new 'Severe Heatwave Warnings' and 'Tropical Night Advisories,' and will send additional disaster alerts for heavy rainfall exceeding 100mm per hour.

articles.description: Following last year's flood, where the Jungnangcheon stream overflowed near Wolgye 1st Bridge in Seoul on August 13, submerging the Dongbu Expressway, new measures are being implemented. Starting this year, township/neighborhood heads will be authorized to issue resident evacuation orders during disasters like heavy rainfall and landslides. Additionally, a 'Severe Heatwave Warning' will be issued when the perceived temperature exceeds 38 degrees Celsius, prompting the suspension of outdoor work and activities. These measures were announced by the government on the 12th as part of its comprehensive disaster response plan.
